---
title: "Week 5: Parallel Iterations & Looping Variants"
author: "Joe Nese"
institute: "University of Oregon<br>Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs: 
    theme: default
    highlight-style: github
    incremental: false
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 3.5
    scrollable: true
    slide-number: true
execute: 
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

```{r}
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
theme_set(theme_minimal(16))
update_geom_defaults("point", list(size = 3))
```

# Parallel Iterations & Looping Variants

**Week 5**

## Agenda {.smaller}

* Discuss `map2_()*` and `pmap_()*` (parallel iterations)
* `walk()` and friends
* `modify()`
* `safely()`
* `reduce()`

**Learning objectives**

* Understand the differences between `map`, `map2`, and `pmap`
* Know when to apply `walk()` instead of `map()`, and why it may be useful
* Understand the similarities and differences between `map()` and `modify()`
* Diagnose errors with `safely()` and understand other situations where it may be helpful
* Collapsing/reducing lists with `purrr::reduce()` or `base::Reduce()`

## Quick Review: `nest_by()` {.smaller}

`dplyr::nest_by()`

- creates a list column for each group, **AND** 
- converts it to a `rowwise` data frame

Think of it as: 

```{r}
#| eval: false
group_by() |> 
  summarise()
```

...and storing the remaining columns as a nested tibble ready for `rowwise()` applications

We'll come back to this

# `map2()`
 
## `map2()`

![](https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/7a545699ff7069a98329fcfbe6e42b734507eb16/211a5/diagrams/functionals/map2-arg.png)

## Two examples

1) Basic simulations - iterating over two vectors

2) Plots by month, changing the title

## 1) Iterating over two vectors

* Simulate data from a normal distribution
    + Vary $n$ from 5 to 150 by increments of 5
  	+ For each $n$, vary $\mu$ (mean) from -2 to 2 by increments of 0.25

. . .

How do we get all combinations?

. . .

`expand.grid()`

## `expand.grid()`

Bonus: It turns it into a data frame!

```{r expand.grid}
ints <- 1:3
lets <- c("a", "b", "c")
expand.grid(ints, lets)
```

## Set conditions

[**Please follow along**]{style="color:#009E73;"}

```{r sim-conditions}
conditions <- expand.grid(
  n = seq(5, 150, 5),
  mu = seq(-2, 2, 0.25)
)
nrow(conditions)
```

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r }
head(conditions)
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r }
tail(conditions)
```
:::

::::

## Simulate!

```{r sim1}
#| output-location: fragment
sim1 <- map2(conditions$n, conditions$mu,
             ~rnorm(n = .x, mean = .y, sd = 10)
             )
str(sim1)
```

## Add it as a list column!

More powerful (this is my preferred method)

```{r list-column-sim1}
#| output-location: fragment
sim2 <- conditions |>
  as_tibble() |> # Not required, but definitely helpful
  mutate(sim = map2(n, mu, ~rnorm(n = .x, mean = .y, sd = 10))) 
sim2
```

## Unnest

```{r list-column-sim2}
#| code-line-numbers: "|4"
#| output-location: fragment
conditions |>
  as_tibble() |> 
  mutate(sim = map2(n, mu, ~rnorm(.x, .y, sd = 10))) |>
  unnest(sim)
```

## Challenge

Can you replicate what we just did, but using a `rowwise()` approach?

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

. . .

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|1|2|3|3"
#| output-location: fragment
conditions |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  mutate(sim = list(rnorm(n, mu, sd = 10))) |> 
  unnest(sim)
```

## Remember {.smaller}

- `mutate()` expects each a vector of the same length as the data frame — one value per row
- `rnorm(n, mu, sd = 10)` returns a vector of length `n`, not a single value
- Without `list()`, `{dplyr}` would try to unpack those `n` values directly into the column, causing a length mismatch error
- Wrapping in `list()` boxes the vector into of length = 1
    + a list with one element
    + so `mutate()` sees exactly one element per row: a length-1 list containing the vector

. . .

With `list()`:

`row 1 → list(c(0.3, -1.2, 0.8)) `  [✓ length 1]{style="color:#009E73;"}

`row 2 → list(c(0.1, 2.4, ...))`    [✓ length 1]{style="color:#009E73;"}

. . .

Without `list()`:

`row 1 → c(0.3, -1.2, 0.8)`         [✗ length 3 — doesn't fit in one row]{style="color:#dd5129;"}

## 2) Plots by month, changing the title

[**Please follow along**]{style="color:#009E73;"}

The data

```{r pulitzer-data}
library(fivethirtyeight)
pulitzer
```

## Prep data

```{r pulitzer-prep}
#| output-location: fragment
pulitzer <- fivethirtyeight::pulitzer |>
  select(newspaper, starts_with("num")) |>
  pivot_longer(
    -newspaper,
    names_to = "year_range",
    values_to = "n",
    names_prefix = "num_finals"
  ) |> 
  mutate(year_range = str_replace_all(year_range, "_", "-")) |>
  filter(year_range != "1990-2014")

head(pulitzer)
```

## One plot {.smaller}

```{r one-plot-pulitzer}
#| output-location: column-fragment
wsj <- pulitzer |>
	filter(newspaper == "Wall Street Journal")

ggplot(wsj, aes(n, year_range)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = n)) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)),
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Pulitzer Prize winners: Wall Street Journal",
    x = "Total number of winners",
    y = ""
  ) 
```

## Nest data

```{r pulitzer-nested}
#| output-location: column
by_newspaper <- pulitzer |>
	group_by(newspaper) |>
	nest()

by_newspaper
```

## Produce all plots

You try first!

Don't worry about the correct title yet

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

## Solution {.smaller}

. . .

Just the plots

```{r pulitzer-plots1}
#| output-location: column
#| code-line-numbers: "|3|4|5-16"
by_newspaper |>
	mutate(
	  plot = map(data, 
	             ~.x |> 
	               ggplot(aes(n, year_range)) +
	               geom_col(aes(fill = n)) +
                 scale_x_continuous(
                   limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)),
                   expand = c(0, 0)
                 ) +
                 guides(fill = "none") +
                 labs(
                   title = "Pulitzer Prize winners",
                   x = "Total number of winners",
                   y = ""
                 )
    )
  )
```

## Add title {.smaller}

. . .

```{r pulitzer-plots2}
#| code-line-numbers: "|5|6|15"
library(glue)

p <- by_newspaper |>
	mutate(
	  plot = map2(data, newspaper, 
	              ~.x |> 
	                ggplot(aes(n, year_range)) +
	                geom_col(aes(fill = n)) +
                  scale_x_continuous(
                    limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)),
                    expand = c(0, 0)
                  ) +
                  guides(fill = "none") +
                  labs(
                    title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: {.y}"), # or paste("Pulitzer Prize winners: ", .y)
                    x = "Total number of winners",
                    y = ""
                  )
	    )
    )
```

##

```{r show-df}
p
```

## Look at a couple plots {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r p1, fig.height = 5.5}
#| output-location: fragment
p$plot[[1]]
p$plot[[3]]
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r p2, fig.height = 5.5}
#| output-location: fragment
p$plot[[2]]
p$plot[[4]]
```
:::

::::

## Challenge

(You can probably guess where this is going)

. . .

Can you reproduce the prior plots using a `rowwise()` approach?

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

## Solution {.smaller}

. . .

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|2|4|5-16|13"
#| output-location: column
pulitzer |>
	nest_by(newspaper) |> 
	mutate(
	  plot = list(
	    ggplot(data, aes(n, year_range)) + 
        geom_col(aes(fill = n)) +
        scale_x_continuous(
          limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)), 
          expand = c(0, 0)
        ) +
        guides(fill = "none") +
        labs(
          title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: {newspaper}"),
          x = "Total number of winners",
          y = ""
        )
    )
  )
```

# `pmap()`

Iterating over $n$ vectors

## 

![](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/diagrams/functionals/pmap-3.png)

. . .

But we can have as many elements `p` as we want

## Simulation

* Simulate data from a normal distribution
  	+ Vary $n$ from 5 to 150 by increments of 5
  	+ For each $n$, vary $\mu$ (mean) from -2 to 2 by increments of 0.25
  	+ For each $n$ and $\mu$, vary each $\sigma$ (*SD*) from 1 to 3 by increments of 0.1

## Simulation

```{r full-conditions}
full_conditions <- expand.grid(
  n = seq(5, 150, 5),
  mu = seq(-2, 2, 0.25),
  sd = seq(1, 3, .1)
)
nrow(full_conditions)
```

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r }
head(full_conditions)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r }
tail(full_conditions)
```
:::

::::

## Full Simulation

```{r full-sim1}
#| output-location: fragment
fsim <- pmap(
  list(
    number = full_conditions$n,
    average = full_conditions$mu,
    stdev = full_conditions$sd
  ), 
  function(number, average, stdev) {
    rnorm(n = number, mean = average, sd = stdev)
  }
)
str(fsim)
```

## Alternative specification

```{r full-sim2}
#| output-location: fragment
fsim <- pmap(
  list(
    full_conditions$n,
    full_conditions$mu,
    full_conditions$sd
  ),
  ~rnorm(n = ..1, mean = ..2, sd = ..3)
)
str(fsim)
```

## Why the `..` prefix?

- `.` or `.x` is used as the placeholder in `map()` 
- `.x` and `.y` are the convention for `map2()`
- So for multi-argument `pmap()`, the `..` prefix is `{purrr}`'s convention for anonymous positional arguments
    + avoid confusion with other functions
    + signals "this is the $p^{th}$ argument in the parallel list"

## Simpler still

Maybe a little too clever

A data frame is a list so...

```{r full-sim3}
#| output-location: fragment
fsim <- pmap(
  full_conditions,
  ~rnorm(n = ..1, mean = ..2, sd = ..3)
)
str(fsim)
```

## List column version

This I like best

```{r fsim-df1}
#| output-location: fragment
full_conditions |>
	as_tibble() |>
	mutate(sim = pmap(list(n, mu, sd), ~rnorm(..1, ..2, ..3)))
```

## Unnest

```{r fsim-df2}
#| output-location: fragment
full_conditions |>
	as_tibble() |>
	mutate(sim = pmap(list(n, mu, sd), ~rnorm(..1, ..2, ..3))) |>
	unnest(sim)
```

## Replicate with `nest_by()`

You try first

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

. . .

```{r }
full_conditions |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  mutate(sim = list(rnorm(n, mu, sd))) |> 
  unnest(sim)
```


## Plot Caption

Add a caption stating the total number of Pulitzer prize winners across years

```{r}
pulitzer
```

## Add column for total Pulitzers

```{r add-col}
pulitzer <- pulitzer |>
	group_by(newspaper) |>
	mutate(tot = sum(n))
pulitzer
```

## Straightfoward approach

Create a column to represent exactly the label you want

```{r add-lab-col}
#| code-line-numbers: "|5|6"
#install.packages("english")
library(english)
pulitzer <- pulitzer |>
	mutate(
	  label = glue(
	    "{str_to_title(as.english(tot))} Total Pulitzer Awards"
    )
)
```

. . .

```{r }
select(pulitzer, newspaper, label)
```

## Produce one plot

```{r plot-cap1}
#| output-location: slide
#| code-line-numbers: "!12,15"

wsj2 <- pulitzer |>
	filter(newspaper == "Wall Street Journal") 

wsj2 |> 
  ggplot(aes(n, year_range)) +
    geom_col(aes(fill = n)) +
    scale_x_continuous(
      limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)),
      expand = c(0, 0)
    ) +
    guides(fill = "none") +
    labs(
      title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: Wall Street Journal"),
      x = "Total number of winners",
      y = "",
      caption = unique(wsj2$label)
    )
```

## Produce all plots

First `nest`

```{r nested}
#| output-location: fragment
by_newspaper_label <- pulitzer |>
	group_by(newspaper, label) |>
	nest() 

by_newspaper_label
```

## Produce plots

```{r plot-cap2}
#| code-line-numbers: "|3|11|14"
final_plots <- by_newspaper_label |>
  mutate(plots = pmap(list(newspaper, label, data), 
    ~ggplot(..3, aes(n, year_range)) +
      geom_col(aes(fill = n)) +
      scale_x_continuous(
        limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)),
        expand = c(0, 0)
      ) +
      guides(fill = "none") +
      labs(
        title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: {..1}"),
        x = "Total number of winners",
        y = "",
        caption = ..2 
      )
  )
)
```

## Produce plots

Re-ordering

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "|2|3|11|14"
final_plots <- by_newspaper_label |>
  mutate(plots = pmap(list(data, newspaper, label), 
    ~ggplot(..1, aes(n, year_range)) +
      geom_col(aes(fill = n)) +
      scale_x_continuous(
        limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)),
        expand = c(0, 0)
      ) +
      guides(fill = "none") +
      labs(
        title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: {..2}"),
        x = "Total number of winners",
        y = "",
        caption = ..3 
      )
  )
)
```

## Look at a couple plots {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r p3, fig.height = 5.5}
final_plots$plots[[1]]
final_plots$plots[[3]]
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r p4, fig.height = 5.5}
final_plots$plots[[2]]
final_plots$plots[[4]]
```
:::
::::

## Replicate with `nest_by()`

You try first

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

## Solution

. . .

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|2|3|5|14"
final_plots2 <- pulitzer |>
  ungroup() |> 
  nest_by(newspaper, label) |> 
	mutate(
	  plots = list(
	    ggplot(data, aes(n, year_range)) + 
	      geom_col(aes(fill = n)) +
	      scale_x_continuous(
	        limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)), 
	        expand = c(0, 0)
        ) +
	      guides(fill = "none") +
	      labs(
	        title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: {newspaper}"),
	        x = "Total number of winners",
	        y = "",
	        caption = label
        )
	  )
)
```

##

```{r }
final_plots2
```

## Save all plots

- We'll have to iterate across at least two things
    1. file path/names 
    2. the plots themselves
- We can do this with the `map()` family, but we'll use a different function
- What are the **steps** we would need to take to do this?
- Could we use a `nest_by()` solution?

## Try with `nest_by()`

You try first:

* Create a vector of file paths
* "loop" through the file paths and the plots to save them

`r countdown::countdown(4)`

## Example {.smaller}

Create a directory

```{r }
fs::dir_create(here::here("plots", "pulitzers"))
```

. . .

Create file paths

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
files <- str_replace_all(
  tolower(final_plots$newspaper),
  " ",
  "-"
)
paths <- here::here("plots", "pulitzers", glue("{files}.png"))
paths
```

## Add paths to data frame

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|2"
final_plots |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  mutate(path = paths) |> 
  select(plots, path)
```

## Save

```{r }
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|2|4|5-10"
final_plots |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  mutate(path = paths) |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  summarize(
    ggsave(path, plots, 
      width = 9.5, 
      height = 6.5,
      dpi = 500
    )
  )
```

## Wrap-up `pmap()`{.smaller}

* Parallel iterations greatly increase the things you can do - iterating through at least two things simultaneously is pretty common
* The `nest_by()` approach can regularly get you the same result as `group_by() |> nest() |> mutate() |> map()`
    + Caveat - must be in a data frame, which means working with list columns
* Probably still worth learning both
    + Looping with `{purrr}` is super flexible and often safer than base versions (type safe) 
    + Doesn't have to be used within a data frame

# Looping variants

## Other loooping fnctions

* `walk()` and friends

* `modify()`

* `safely()`

* `reduce()`

## What we want to know

* Know when to apply `walk()` instead of `map()`, and why it may be useful
* Understand the parallels and differences between `map()` and `modify()`
* Diagnose errors with `safely()` and understand other situations where it may be helpful
* Collapsing/reducing lists with `purrr::reduce()` or `base::Reduce()`

## Setup

Let's go back to our plotting example:

**Saving**

* We saw last time that we could use `nest_by()`
    + Required a bit of awkwardness with adding the paths to the data frame

* Instead, we'll do it again but with the `walk()` family


## Why `walk()`? {.smaller}

- `walk()` is an alternative to `map` that you use when you want to call a function for its side effects, **NOT** than for its return value
- You typically do this because you want to render output to the screen or save files to disk
- The important thing is the action, not the return value

::: aside
[r4ds](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/iteration.html#walk)
:::

## More practical

- If you use `walk()`, nothing will get printed to the screen
- This is particularly helpful for quarto files
- Most often use it for
    1. saving
    2. printing

## Example {visibility="hidden"}

[**Please do the following**]{style="color:#009E73;"}

* Create a new Quarto document
* Paste the code you have for creating the Pulitzer plots in a code chunk there
    + see next slide

## `final_plots` code {visibility="hidden"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(fivethirtyeight)
library(english)
library(tidyverse)
library(glue)

pulitzer <- fivethirtyeight::pulitzer |>
  select(newspaper, starts_with("num")) |>
  pivot_longer(
    -newspaper,
    names_to = "year_range",
    values_to = "n",
    names_prefix = "num_finals"
  ) |> 
  mutate(year_range = str_replace_all(year_range, "_", "-")) |>
  filter(year_range != "1990-2014") |>
  group_by(newspaper) |>
  mutate(tot = sum(n)) |>
  mutate(
    label = glue(
      "{str_to_title(as.english(tot))} Total Pulitzer Awards"
    )
  )

by_newspaper_label <- pulitzer |>
  group_by(newspaper, label) |>
  nest()

final_plots <- by_newspaper_label |>
  mutate(plots = pmap(list(newspaper, label, data), 
                      ~ggplot(..3, aes(n, year_range)) +
                        geom_col(aes(fill = n)) +
                        scale_x_continuous(
                          limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)),
                          expand = c(0, 0)
                        ) +
                        guides(fill = "none") +
                        labs(
                          title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: {..1}"),
                          x = "Total number of winners",
                          y = "",
                          caption = ..2 
                        )
                      )
  )
```

## Create a directory {visibility="hidden"}

We already did this, but in case we hadn't...

```{r }
fs::dir_create(here::here("plots", "pulitzers"))
```

## Create file paths {visibility="hidden"}

```{r }
newspapers <- str_replace_all(
  tolower(final_plots$newspaper),
  " ",
  "-"
)
paths <- here::here(
  "plots",
  "pulitzers",
  glue("{newspapers}.png")
)
```

## Challenge {visibility="hidden"}

* Use a `map()` family function to loop through `paths` and `final_plots$plots` to save all plots
* Render your file. What do you notice?

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

## `walk()` family

- Just like `map()`, we have parallel variants of `walk()`, including, `walk2()`, and `pwalk()`
- These work just like `map()` but don't print to the screen

## Let's make a list column

```{r}
final_plots
```

## Let's make a list column

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
final_plots3 <- final_plots |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  mutate(
    files = str_replace_all(tolower(newspaper), " ", "-"),
    path = paste0(here::here("plots", "pulitzers", paste0(files, ".png")))
  )
```

## Saving plots {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
`map()`

```{r }
map2(final_plots3$path[1:4], final_plots3$plots[1:4], ggsave)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
`walk()`

```{r }
walk2(final_plots3$path[1:4], final_plots3$plots[1:4], ggsave)
```
:::

::::

## `modify()`

- `map()` family always return a fixed object type
    + list for `map()`
    + integer vector for `map_int()`
    + etc.
- `modify()` family always returns the same type as the input object

## `map()` vs `modify()`

`map()`

```{r map-mtcars}
map(mtcars, ~as.numeric(scale(.x)))
```

## `map()` vs `modify()`

`modify()`

```{r modify-mtcars}
modify(mtcars, ~as.numeric(scale(.x)))
```

## `map()` vs `modify()` {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
map(mtcars, ~as.numeric(scale(.x))) |> 
  str()
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
modify(mtcars, ~as.numeric(scale(.x))) |> 
  str()
```
:::

::::
## `map()` vs `modify()`

```{r map-paste}
map2(LETTERS[1:3], letters[1:3], paste0)
modify2(LETTERS[1:3], letters[1:3], paste0)
```

## `safely()`

Errors during iterations

- Sometimes a loop will work for most cases, but return an error on a few
- Often, you want to return the output you can
- Alternatively, you might want to diagnose *where* the error is occurring

. . .

`purrr::safely()`

## Example

[**Please run this code**]{style="color:#009E73;"}

```{r by_cyl}
by_cyl <- mpg |> 
  group_by(cyl) |> 
  nest() 
by_cyl
```

`r countdown::countdown(1)`

## Now try to fit a model

[**Please follow along**]{style="color:#009E73;"}

Notice the error message is *super* helpful!

```{r lm-fail}
#| error: true
by_cyl |> 
  mutate(mod = map(data, ~lm(hwy ~ displ + drv, data = .x)))
```

## Safe return

First, define safe function - note that this will work for **ANY** function

```{r safe-lm}
safe_lm <- safely(lm)
```

. . .

Next, loop the safe function, instead of the standard function

```{r loop-safe-lm}
#| code-line-numbers: "|2"
#| output-location: fragment
safe_models <- by_cyl |> 
  mutate(safe_mod = map(data, ~safe_lm(hwy ~ displ + drv, data = .x)))
safe_models
```

## What's returned? {.smaller}

```{r safe-return}
#| output-location: fragment
safe_models$safe_mod[[1]]
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
safe_models$safe_mod[[4]]
```

## Inspecting

I often use `safely()` to help me de-bug

Why is it failing *there* (but note the error message here helps with this too)

. . .

Create a new variable to filter for results with errors

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
safe_models |> 
  mutate(error = map_lgl(safe_mod, ~!is.null(.x$error)))
```

## Inspecting the data

```{r }
safe_models |> 
  mutate(error = map_lgl(safe_mod, ~!is.null(.x$error))) |> 
  filter(error) |> 
  select(cyl, data) |> 
  unnest(data)
```

. . .

`lm(hwy ~ displ + drv)`

The `displ` and `drv` variables are constant, so no relation can be estimated (no variance)

## Pull results that worked

```{r results}
safe_models |> 
  mutate(results = map(safe_mod, "result"))
```

## Pull results that worked

Now we can `broom::tidy()` or similar

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|3|4|5"
#| output-location: fragment
safe_models |> 
  mutate(results = map(safe_mod, "result"),
         tidied = map(results, broom::tidy)) |> 
  select(cyl, tidied) |> 
  unnest(tidied)
```

## When else might we use this?

How about web scraping - pages change and URLs don't always work

## Example

```{r}
#| error: true
library(rvest)
links <- list(
  "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FC_Barcelona",
  "https://nosuchpage",
  "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rome"
)

pages <- map(links, ~{
  Sys.sleep(0.1)
  read_html(.x)
})

```

## The problem

I can't connect to <https://nosuchpage> because it doesn't exist

. . .

**BUT** 

. . .

That also means I can't get *any* of my links because **one** page errored 

Imagine it was 1 in 1,000 instead of 1 in 3!


## `safely()` to the rescue

Safe version

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|1|4"
#| output-location: fragment
safe_read_html <- safely(read_html)
pages <- map(links, ~{
  Sys.sleep(0.1)
  safe_read_html(.x)
})
str(pages)
```

## Non-results

In a real example, we'd probably want to double-check the pages where we got no results

```{r }
errors <- map_lgl(pages, ~!is.null(.x$error))
links[errors]
```


## `reduce()`

Reducing a list

- The `map()` family of functions will always return a vector the same length as the input
- `reduce()` will collapse or reduce the list to a single element

## Example

```{r }
l <- list(
  c(1, 3),
  c(1, 5, 7, 9),
  3,
  c(4, 8, 12, 2)
)
```

. . .

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}

`map()`

```{r }
map(l, sum)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}

`reduce()`

```{r }
reduce(l, sum)
```

:::

::::

## What's going on?

The code `reduce(l, sum)` is the same as

```{r }
sum(l[[4]], sum(l[[3]], sum(l[[1]], l[[2]])))
```

Or slightly differently

```{r }
first_sum <- sum(l[[1]], l[[2]])
second_sum <- sum(first_sum, l[[3]])
final_sum <- sum(second_sum, l[[4]])
final_sum
```

## Why might you use this? {.smaller}

What if you had a list of data frames like this

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
(l_df <- list(
  tibble(id = 1:3, score = rnorm(3)),
  tibble(id = 1:5, treatment = rbinom(5, 1, .5)),
  tibble(id = c(1, 3, 5, 7), other_thing = rnorm(4))
))
```

## Why might you use this?

We can join these all together with a single loop

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
reduce(l_df, full_join)
```

## Caution

You have to be careful on directionality

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
`left_join()`

```{r }
reduce(l_df, left_join)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
`right_join()`

```{r }
reduce(l_df, right_join)
```
:::

::::

## Another example {.smaller}

If you have the same columns, you probably just want to `bind_rows()`

```{r }
(l_df2 <- list(
  tibble(id = 1:3, scid = 1, score = rnorm(3)),
  tibble(id = 1:5, scid = 2, score = rnorm(5)),
  tibble(id = c(1, 3, 5, 7), scid = 3, score = rnorm(4))
))
```

. . .

```{r }
reduce(l_df2, bind_rows)
```

## Non-loop version

Luckily, the prior slide has become obsolete, because `bind_rows()` will do the list reduction for us

```{r }
bind_rows(l_df2)
```

## Another example

This is a poor example, but there are use cases like this

```{r }
library(palmerpenguins)
map(penguins, as.character) |> 
  reduce(paste)
```

## Why use `reduce()`

- This is one that is regularly used, but I have a hard time coming up with good examples for it
- The `{tidyverse}` makes it less needed, generally
- Still a good "tool" to have

## Wrap up

* Lots more to `{purrr}` but we've covered a lot
* Functional programming can *really* help your efficiency, and even if it slows you down initially, I'd recommend always striving toward it, because it will ultimately be a huge help

# Next time

## Before next class

- Readings
    + [Creating Functions (SC)](https://swcarpentry.github.io/r-novice-inflammation/02-func-R.html)
    + [AdvR(2e) 6.1-6.4](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/functions.html)
- Midterm Test

# Midterm Test
